incorporations.reason: Italian 'colpi di corda' is closer to 'lashes' (blows delivered by a rope) than 'strokes', which sounds more like caning

incorporations.original: That villainy was so entirely out of place
incorporations.replacement: That piece of coarseness was so entirely uncalled for
incorporations.reason: Italian 'villania' means coarseness or rudeness, not villainy in the English sense of evil scheming; 'uncalled for' better renders 'fuor di proposito' than 'out of place'
Italian 'reprimere' maps directly to 'repress'; Gemini used this verb

incorporations.original: some fifty convicts who formed my gang
incorporations.replacement: some fifty convicts who formed my squad
incorporations.reason: 'Squad' better renders 'squadra' and avoids the criminal connotation of 'gang', which would colour the narrator's tone inappropriately
Italian 'le ossa rotte' is idiomatic for exhaustion, not literal fracture; 'aching' conveys the intended sense more naturally in English
'Tronchi' in context (pieces to be carried on shoulders) are logs, not whole tree-trunks; 'logs' is more concise and fits the labour described
'Trips' is more colloquial and appropriate for repeated back-and-forth portage than 'journeys', which implies greater distance

incorporations.original: your skull will no longer see the sun from the top of your shoulders
incorporations.replacement: your pumpkin will see the sun no more from atop your shoulders
incorporations.reason: Italian 'zucca' literally means pumpkin/gourd; keeping the vegetable metaphor preserves the overseer's mocking register

incorporations.original: the clerk of the superintending Engineer
incorporations.replacement: the clerk of the supervising Engineer
incorporations.reason: 'Soprintendente' is closer to 'supervising' than to the more formal 'superintending'

incorporations.original: sooner or later you are done for!
incorporations.replacement: sooner or later, you are a dead man!
incorporations.reason: Italian 'sei spacciato' is blunter than 'done for'; 'you are a dead man' preserves the direct menace of the clerk's warning
Italian 'placidamente' maps to 'placidly' more precisely than 'peacefully'; retains the Latinate register

incorporations.original: Each convict ran to his own and ate
incorporations.replacement: Each convict ran to his own and devoured it
incorporations.reason: Italian 'divorava' means devoured, not merely ate; preserving the stronger verb conveys the hunger of the convicts
Italian 'feroce' is 'ferocious'; the stronger adjective suits the extremity of the narrator's hunger after forced labour

incorporations.original: to deceive any eyes that might still be open
incorporations.replacement: to deceive any eyes that might still be awake
incorporations.reason: Italian 'desti' means awake, not open; eyes being 'awake' implies alert watchfulness rather than merely open lids

incorporations.original: threw the entire penal colony into confusion
incorporations.replacement: threw the entire penal colony into turmoil
incorporations.reason: Italian 'sconvolse' conveys violent upheaval; 'turmoil' is stronger than 'confusion' and closer to the original force

incorporations.original: The catastrophe was rapid beyond all telling
incorporations.replacement: The catastrophe was swift beyond all telling
incorporations.reason: Italian 'rapida' can be either, but 'swift' has a more literary register that suits the passage's tone

edgren_influences.note: Edgren confirms 'soverchio' means 'excessive' rather than the weaker 'undue' or 'particular'; both drafts had this right, and the final retains it

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: instead; on the contrary; rather, nay; before
edgren_influences.note: Edgren lists 'nay' (used by Claude draft) and 'rather/on the contrary'. The final chose 'Indeed' to avoid the archaic 'Nay' while preserving the intensifying force Edgren documents; a compromise between the drafts

Edgren gives 'derelict (abandoned)' as the core sense. Gemini used 'derelicts' literally; the final preferred 'wretches' (from the Claude draft) because the Italian usage here emphasises pitiable condition more than abandonment
Edgren confirms the geographical sense directly; both drafts and the final use it
Edgren's entry for 'reale' confirms the primary sense is 'royal'; retained in the French place-name rather than translating to English
